(lit. 'our language'), also known as **Tornedalian**,[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) is a [Finnic language](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") or a group of distinct [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") dialects spoken in the northernmost part of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), particularly along the [Torne River Valley](/wiki/Torne_River_Valley "Torne River Valley"). It is officially recognized in Sweden as one of the [country's five minority languages](/wiki/Official_minority_languages_of_Sweden "Official minority languages of Sweden") and is treated as a separate language from Finnish. According to the National Association of Swedish Tornedalians, 70,000 individuals understand Meänkieli, at least to some level. Most fluent speakers are aged 65 or older.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

**Meänkieli** (lit. 'our language'), also known as **Tornedalian**,[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) is a [Finnic language](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") or a group of distinct [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") dialects spoken in the northernmost part of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), particularly along the [Torne River Valley](/wiki/Torne_River_Valley "Torne River Valley"). It is officially recognized in Sweden as one of the [country's five minority languages](/wiki/Official_minority_languages_of_Sweden "Official minority languages of Sweden") and is treated as a separate language from Finnish. According to the National Association of Swedish Tornedalians, 70,000 individuals understand Meänkieli, at least to some level. Most fluent speakers are aged 65 or older.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Meänkieli is particularly similar to the [Kven language](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language") and the [Peräpohjola dialects](/wiki/Per%C3%A4pohjola_dialects "Peräpohjola dialects") of Finnish spoken in Finland, and it is strongly mutually intelligible with them. Its status as an independent language is sometimes disputed due to this high degree of mutual intelligibility.[[5]](#cite_note-5) However, Meänkieli contains strong influences from [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) a number of loanwords from the [Sámi languages](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages"),[[7]](#cite_note-:07-7) preserves some archaic features that even the northern Finnish dialects have lost, and lacks the changes which standard Finnish experienced in the 19th to 20th centuries.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) As a result, while Meänkieli is often intelligible to speakers of Finnish, standard Finnish is often very difficult for speakers of Meänkieli to understand.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [Gällivare varieties](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare_dialects "Gällivare dialects") of Meänkieli differ even more significantly from standard Finnish and from other Finnish dialects, notably particularly their complete absence of [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony").[[11]](#cite_note-:5-11)

A written Meänkieli language has been developed since the 1970s.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Me%C3%A4nkieli&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Before 1809, all of what is today Finland was an integral part of Sweden. The language border went west of the Torne Valley area, so the upper section of today's Sweden (about 10% by area), was historically [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") speaking (just like most areas along the eastern coast of the southern part of [Gulf of Bothnia](/wiki/Gulf_of_Bothnia "Gulf of Bothnia"), areas that were [ceded](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fredrikshamn "Treaty of Fredrikshamn") to Russia and are part of modern Finland, were historically [Swedish speaking](/wiki/Swedish-speaking_Finns "Swedish-speaking Finns"), and to a large extent still are). The area where Meänkieli is spoken that is now northern Sweden (apart from the linguistically [Sami](/wiki/Sami_languages "Sami languages") and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") parts of this geographical area), formed a [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") within the [Realm of Sweden](/wiki/Realm_of_Sweden "Realm of Sweden"). Since the area east of Torne River was ceded to [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") in 1809, the language spoken on the western side of it developed in partial isolation from [standard](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") Finnish. In 1826, the state [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden") appointed the priest and amateur botanist [Lars Levi Laestadius](/wiki/Lars_Levi_Laestadius "Lars Levi Laestadius") to be the [Vicar](/wiki/Vicar "Vicar") over the [Karesuando](/wiki/Karesuando "Karesuando") parish, which is situated along the [Muonio River](/wiki/Muonio_(river) "Muonio (river)") north of the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle") on the border of Finland in [Swedish Lapland](/wiki/Lapland_(Sweden) "Lapland (Sweden)").

In the 1880s, the Swedish state decided that all citizens of the country should speak Swedish. Part of the reason was military; people close to the border speaking the language of the neighbouring country rather than the major language in their own country might not be trusted in case of war. Another reason was that Finns were sometimes regarded as being of another "race." An opinion of that period, as reflected in contemporary fiction, was that the Sami and the Finnish populations belonged "more closely to Russia than to Scandinavia".[[12]](#cite_note-12) Beginning around this time, the schools in the area only taught in Swedish, and children were forbidden under penalty of physical punishment from speaking their own language at school even during class breaks. Native Finnish speakers were prevented by the authorities from learning Standard Finnish as a school subject for decades, which resulted in the survival of the language only in oral form.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Me%C3%A4nkieli_varieteettikartta_2.png/250px-Me%C3%A4nkieli_varieteettikartta_2.png)](/wiki/File:Me%C3%A4nkieli_varieteettikartta_2.png)

Traditional spread of the Western Peräpohja variants in both Sweden and Finland. The [Torne-Valley dialects](/wiki/Torne_Valley_dialects "Torne Valley dialects") are in blue, [Lannankieli](/wiki/Lannankieli "Lannankieli") in green and [Gällivare dialects](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare_dialects "Gällivare dialects") in yellow.

When minority languages first became taught in Swedish schools, Meänkieli was still classified by Sweden as a form of Finnish and children who spoke it natively were taught the standard Finnish literary language in school instead of their more native Tornedalian variant, causing the students to become demotivated. However, in 1977 some schools began to make materials specifically designed for speakers of Meänkieli which did not use standard Finnish. This was also the first time the term "Meänkieli" was applied to the form of speech, which was called "Tornedalian Finnish" before.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Meänkieli today

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Me%C3%A4nkieli&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Meänkieli today")]

On April 1, 2000, Meänkieli became one of the now five nationally recognized [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_languages_in_Sweden "Minority languages in Sweden") of Sweden, which means it can be used for some communication with local and regional authorities in the communities along the Finnish border. Its minority language status applies in designated local communities and areas, not throughout Sweden.

Few people today speak Meänkieli as their only language, with speakers usually knowing Swedish and often standard Finnish as well. Estimates of how many people speak Meänkieli vary from 30,000 to 70,000, of whom most live in [Norrbotten](/wiki/Norrbotten_County "Norrbotten County"). Many people in the northern parts of Sweden understand some Meänkieli, but fewer people speak it regularly. People with Meänkieli roots are often referred to as [Tornedalians](/wiki/Tornedalians "Tornedalians") although the Finnish-speaking part of Norrbotten is a far larger area than the [Torne River Valley](/wiki/Torne_River_Valley "Torne River Valley"); the Meänkieli-speaking part of Norrbotten stretches as far west as [Gällivare municipality](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare_Municipality "Gällivare Municipality").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Today Meänkieli is declining. Few young people speak Meänkieli as part of daily life though many have passive knowledge of the language from family use, and it is not uncommon for younger people from Meänkieli-speaking families to be more familiar with standard Finnish, for which literature and courses are much more readily available. The language is taught at [Stockholm University](/wiki/Stockholm_University "Stockholm University"), [Luleå University of Technology](/wiki/Lule%C3%A5_University_of_Technology "Luleå University of Technology"), [Umeå University](/wiki/Ume%C3%A5_University "Umeå University"), and [Babeș-Boylai University](/wiki/Babe%C8%99-Bolyai_University "Babeș-Bolyai University") in Romania.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 2020, [Oulu University](/wiki/University_of_Oulu "University of Oulu") began teaching translators in Meänkieli and Kven.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Bengt Pohjanen](/wiki/Bengt_Pohjanen "Bengt Pohjanen") is a trilingual author from the Torne Valley. In 1985 he wrote the first Meänkieli novel, *Lyykeri*. He has also written several novels, dramas, grammar books, songs and films in Meänkieli.

The author [Mikael Niemi](/wiki/Mikael_Niemi "Mikael Niemi")'s novels and a film based on one of his books in Swedish have improved awareness of this minority among Swedes. Since the 1980s, people who speak Meänkieli have become more aware of the importance of the language as a marker of identity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Today there are grammar books, a Bible translation, drama performances, and there are some TV programmes in Meänkieli.

On radio, programmes in Meänkieli are broadcast regularly from regional station [P4 Norrbotten](/wiki/Sveriges_Radio_P4 "Sveriges Radio P4") (as well as local station P6 in Stockholm) on Mondays to Thursdays between 17:10 and 18:00, while on Sundays further programmes are carried by P6 between 8:34 and 10:00 (also on [P2](/wiki/Sveriges_Radio_P2 "Sveriges Radio P2") nationwide from 8:34 to 9:00). All of these programmes are also available via the Internet.

## Status as a language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Me%C3%A4nkieli&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Status as a language")]

Individuals who support the classification of Meänkieli as a separate language generally do so for historical, political and sociological reasons. They often point to its separate history, cultural significance, official status, and its standardised written language which differs from Standard Finnish. According to Harri Mantila, a professor at the [University of Oulu](/wiki/University_of_Oulu "University of Oulu"), the recognition as an independent language is important to many of the speakers. It has increased the [linguistic prestige](/wiki/Linguistic_prestige "Linguistic prestige") of Meänkieli which has been historically very low due to the process of [Swedification](/wiki/Swedification "Swedification"), thus helping to strengthen the cultural identity for the [Tornedalians](/wiki/Tornedalians "Tornedalians").[[17]](#cite_note-:3-17)

On linguistic grounds, Meänkieli may be classified as a group of Northern [Peräpohjola dialects](/wiki/Per%C3%A4pohjola_dialects "Peräpohjola dialects") of Finnish, traditionally spoken on the Finnish side of the [Torne River](/wiki/Torne_(Finnish_and_Swedish_river) "Torne (Finnish and Swedish river)"). The establishment of the 1809 border led Meänkieli to evolve separately, resulting in increased Swedish influence and the preservation of some archaic features. Swedish has influenced the syntax and the phonetics of Meänkieli, and [code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") between Meänkieli and Swedish is common among its speakers. The language also includes many Swedish loan words.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-18) Despite these differences, Meänkieli remains largely intelligible to Finnish speakers, partially due to the fact that [Swedish is a mandatory subject](/wiki/Mandatory_Swedish "Mandatory Swedish") in Finnish schools, making the heavy Swedish elements in Meänkieli less of a barrier for comprehension.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-17)

One key distinction between Meänkieli speakers and native Finnish speakers is their use of [linguistic registers](/wiki/Linguistic_register "Linguistic register"). Native Finns typically alternate between a [spoken dialect](/wiki/Colloquial_Finnish "Colloquial Finnish") and the standardised written form of Finnish, which is taught in schools.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In contrast, during the period of minority language suppression in Swedish schools, Meänkieli was confined in private use, and its speakers did not learn Standard Finnish.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-17) In addition, Standard Finnish has [developed thousands of neologisms](/wiki/Finnish_language#Neologisms "Finnish language") to replace foreign words—many of which are unintelligible to Meänkieli speakers.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-18) When Finnish-language education resumed in the early 1970s, it became clear that students struggled with materials written in Standard Finnish. Learning outcomes began to improve only when after instruction began to be delivered in local dialects and efforts to develop a written language were initiated.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-17) As of 2010, the written standard was still under development and had not yet gained widespread recognition.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-18)

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Me%C3%A4nkieli&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Me%C3%A4nkieli_varieteettikartta.png/250px-Me%C3%A4nkieli_varieteettikartta.png)](/wiki/File:Me%C3%A4nkieli_varieteettikartta.png)

Traditional spread of the Meänkieli variants

In Sweden, Meänkieli consists of three dialect subgroups, the [Torne Valley dialects](/wiki/Torne_Valley_dialects "Torne Valley dialects") (spoken in [Pajala](/wiki/Pajala_Municipality "Pajala Municipality"), [Övertorneå](/wiki/%C3%96vertorne%C3%A5_Municipality "Övertorneå Municipality"), [Haparanda](/wiki/Haparanda_Municipality "Haparanda Municipality") and parts of [Kiruna](/wiki/Kiruna_Municipality "Kiruna Municipality")), [Lannankieli](/wiki/Lannankieli "Lannankieli") (spoken in Kiruna municipality) and the [Gällivare dialects](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare_dialects "Gällivare dialects") (spoken in [Gällivare municipality](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare_Municipality "Gällivare Municipality")), which all descend from the larger [Peräpohjola dialect](/wiki/Per%C3%A4pohjola_dialects "Peräpohjola dialects") group.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) The Torne Valley dialects are the most major variant group of Meänkieli, and the written standard language of Meänkieli is primarily derived from the Torne Valley dialects spoken in Pajala and Övertorneå.[[22]](#cite_note-22) However, the Lannankieli and Gällivare variants are more severely endangered.[[23]](#cite_note-23) All three dialect groups are mutually intelligible with each other, however they contain some lexical differences.[[24]](#cite_note-:12-24)

## Geographical distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Me%C3%A4nkieli&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geographical distribution")]

Meänkieli has an official status in: [Pajala](/wiki/Pajala_Municipality "Pajala Municipality"), [Övertorneå](/wiki/%C3%96vertorne%C3%A5 "Övertorneå"), [Haparanda](/wiki/Haparanda "Haparanda"), [Gällivare](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare "Gällivare"), [Luleå](/wiki/Lule%C3%A5 "Luleå"), [Kalix](/wiki/Kalix_Municipality "Kalix Municipality"), [Kiruna](/wiki/Kiruna_Municipality "Kiruna Municipality"), [Umeå](/wiki/Ume%C3%A5_Municipality "Umeå Municipality") and [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Meänkieli has also been historically spoken in [Piteå](/wiki/Pite%C3%A5_Municipality "Piteå Municipality"), [Boden](/wiki/Boden_Municipality "Boden Municipality"), [Älvsbyn](/wiki/%C3%84lvsbyn_Municipality "Älvsbyn Municipality") and northeastern [Jokkmokk municipality](/wiki/Jokkmokk_Municipality "Jokkmokk Municipality").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Me%C3%A4nkieli&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Meänkieli grammar](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli_grammar "Meänkieli grammar")

The grammar of Meänkieli is very similar to Finnish, with some variations such as the aspiration of consonants before long vowels. Meänkieli is an agglutinative language with fifteen noun cases. It contains consonant gradation and vowel harmony, just like Finnish. It contains four verb tenses, which are the present, imperfect, perfect and the pluperfect. It does not have a separate tense for future events.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Differences between standard Finnish and Meänkieli

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Me%C3%A4nkieli&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Differences between standard Finnish and Meänkieli")]

**1) There exists often either the omission of the *d* sound (in native words) or its replacement with *t* (in loanwords):[[33]](#cite_note-:06-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)**

* *tehä* 'to do' (standard Finnish: tehdä)
* *tynamiitti* 'dynamite' (standard Finnish: dynamiitti)
* *syyä* 'to eat' (standard Finnish: syödä,)
* *meän* 'our' (standard Finnish: meidän,)
* *teän* 'your' (standard Finnish: teidän)
* *heän* 'their' (standard Finnish: heidän)
* *soan* 'of the war' (standard Finnish: sodan)

**2) In certain environments, gemination (doubling of consonants) occurs, which differs from standard Finnish:[[35]](#cite_note-35)**

Instead of *ts* clusters, there is *tt* (similar to western Finnish dialects):

* *mettä* 'forest' (standard Finnish: metsä)
* *kattoa* 'to look' (standard Finnish: katsoa)

Some consonant clusters have assimilated into geminates:

*jokka* 'who' (standard Finnish: jotka)

Meänkieli often has the geminate *vv*:

* *savvu* 'smoke' (Finnish: savu)
* *avvain* 'key' (standard Finnish: avain)

**3) Verb conjugation endings: -mma ~ -mmä, -tta ~ -ttä, -pi[[36]](#cite_note-:05-36)**

* *menemmä* 'we go' (standard Finnish: menemme)
* *tuletta* 'you come' (standard Finnish: tulette)
* *ostaapi* 'buys' (standard Finnish: ostaa)
* *syövä* 'they eat' (standard Finnish: syövät)

**4) Past participle:[[37]](#cite_note-:04-37)**

The standard Finnish *syönyt* (pronounced, *syöny* or *syönny*) form corresponds to *syönny* in Meänkieli (not present in all dialects).

**5) In certain loanwords, Meänkieli has the sound *y* under the influence of Swedish, while Finnish uses the sound *u*:[[38]](#cite_note-38)**

* *kylttyyri* = 'culture' (Finnish: kulttuuri)
* *mysiikki* = 'music' (Finnish: musiikki)
* *resyrssi* = 'resource' (Finnish: resurssi)

**6) In loanwords, Meänkieli has preserved the *f* sound, whereas in Finnish it has often become *v*:[[39]](#cite_note-39)**

* *färi* 'color' (standard Finnish: väri)
* *fankila* 'prison' (Finnish: vankila)
* *fati* 'bowl' (standard Finnish: vati)
* *fiuletti* 'violet' (standard Finnish: violetti)

**7) In recent loanwords, Meänkieli often uses the sound *u* under the influence of Swedish, while Finnish uses the sound *o*:[[40]](#cite_note-40)**

* *puliisi* 'police' (Finnish: poliisi)
* *muterni* 'modern' (Finnish: moderni)
* *pulitiikki* 'politics' (Finnish: politiikka)
* *pulitiikkeri* 'politician' (Finnish: politiikko)
* *vukaali* 'vowel' (Finnish: vokaali)
* *pusitiivinen* 'positive' (Finnish: positiivinen)

**8) The verb *olla* (to be) is sometimes combined with personal pronouns in the spoken form of Meänkieli:[[41]](#cite_note-41)**

* *Molen* = I am (standard Finnish: olen)
* *Solet* = you are (standard Finnish: olet)
* *Son* = it is, he/she is (standard Finnish: hän on)
* *Sole* = it is not (standard Finnish: se ei ole)
* *Molema* = we are (standard Finnish: me olemme)
* *Toletta* = you (plural) are (standard Finnish: te olette)
* *Non/Noova/Hoova* = they are (standard Finnish: he ovat)

**9) Meänkieli often uses the ending *-tten* in plural genitives:[[42]](#cite_note-:03-42)**

* *kaloitten* 'of the fish' (Finnish: kalojen)
* *miehitten* 'of the men' (Finnish: miesten)
* *taloitten* 'of the houses' (Finnish: talojen)
* *asunnoitten* 'of the apartments' (Finnish: asuntojen)

**10) Personal pronouns.[[42]](#cite_note-:03-42)**

Personal pronouns in Meänkieli somewhat differ from those used in standard Finnish:

|  | Meänkieli | Finnish | Colloquial Finnish |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1st person singular | mie | minä | mä/mie |
| 2nd person singular | sie | sinä | sä/sie |
| 3rd person singular | hään/se | hän/se | se |
| 1st person plural | met | me | me |
| 2nd person plural | tet | te | te |
| 3rd person plural | het | he/ne | ne |

**11) Meänkieli often uses the "š" sound in loanwords due to Swedish influence.[[42]](#cite_note-:03-42)**

* šinkka = ham
* informašuuni = information
* lekitimašuuni = personal identification

**12) Meänkieli uses -sti first personal possessive instead of the Finnish -si ending.**[[43]](#cite_note-43)

* piilisti = your car (standard Finnish: autosi)
* pappasti = your father (standard Finnish: isäsi)
* talhoosti = into your house (standard Finnish; taloosi)

### Gällivare dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Me%C3%A4nkieli&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Gällivare dialects")]

The [Gällivare dialects](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare_dialects "Gällivare dialects") contain multiple further differences from standard Finnish, that make it unique among the variants of Meänkieli. The Gällivare dialects often differ very radically from standard Finnish in many ways. For instance, the Gällivare dialects completely lack vowel harmony, which is seen in words such as *kyla* 'village', *päiva* 'day' and *näkkuu* 'is seen', which in standard Finnish are kylä, päivä and näkyy. The Gällivare dialect also contains many triphtongs, such as *syöi* 'ate', *juoi* 'drank', *viei* 'took', which in standard Finnish are *söi*, *joi* and *vei*. Gällivare also uses the passive form of verbs in the third person plural, thus the Gällivare sentence *tyttäret juoshin* 'the daughters ran', in standard Finnish is *tytöt juoksivat*.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-11)

Rödlyyva sai ruokakorin ja lääkheet matkhaan ja äiti muistutti tyärtä ette marsia suoraa tietä mummun tykö eikä topata välilä praatimhaan kenenkhän kans. | Olipa kerran pieni tyttö joka oli saanut hienon punaisen hilkan kiltiltä mummoltaan. Siksi tyttöä kutsuttiin Punahilkaksi. Yhtenä päivänä Punahilkan äiti käski hänen mennä mummon luokse, koska mummo makasi sairaana pienessä mökissään pimeässä synkässä metsässä. Punahilkka sai ruokakorin ja lääkkeet mukaansa ja äiti muistutti tytärtä, että kulkee suoraa tietä mummon luokse eikä pysähdy välillä puhumaan kenenkään kanssa. | Det var en gång en liten flicka, som hade fått en fin röd huva av sin snälla mormor. Därför kallades flickan Rödluvan. En dag bad mamman att flickan skulle gå till mormor, som låg sjuk i sin stuga djupt inne i skogen. Flickan fick en korg med mat och medicin att ta med. Mamma förmanade flickan att gå raka vägen till mormor och inte stanna och prata med någon på vägen. | Once upon a time, there was a little girl who had received a beautiful red hood from her kind grandmother. That’s why the girl was called Little Red Riding Hood. One day, the girl's mother asked her to go to her grandmother, who was sick in her cottage deep in the forest. Little Red Riding Hood was given a basket with food and medicine to take with her. Mother warned the girl to go straight to grandmother's and not stop to talk to anyone along the way. |
